data.title: Transcontinental Should Benefit from Price Controls, M&A — Market Talk
data.description: Transcontinental's retail services and print segment remains in secular decline, but Scotiabank's Maher Yaghi says cost controls and acquisitions should help offset the strain. The company recently sold off its packaging segment and paid out a C$20-a-share special dividend with the proceeds. Scotiabank downgrades the share price target to C$6 from C$26 to reflect the new scale of the business. "We value that business at 4x EV/EBITDA as the segment remains in secular decline but profitability should be helped by cost reductions and M&A," Yaghi says. Shares are trading 11% higher at C$5.33. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: EU antitrust chief meets Google, Meta, OpenAI, Amazon CEOs amidst AI scrutiny
data.description:
By Foo Yun Chee

EU antitrust chief Teresa Ribera, who has voiced concerns about Big Tech extending its dominance into artificial intelligence, will meet the CEOs of Alphabet , Meta Platforms and OpenAI on Tuesday, a European Commission agenda item shows.

The first-time meetings with Sundar Pichai, Mark Zuckerberg, and Sam Altman in San Francisco will take place as part of Ribera's week-long trip to the United States where she is scheduled to speak at an American Bar Association conference on Friday.

She will also meet Amazon CEO Andy Jassy on Wednesday.

Ribera, who has launched several investigations into Google and Meta's business practices, this month said she was examining the entire AI stack, including AI chatbots, the data used to train them and the cloud computing infrastructure.

The European Commission, which acts as the EU competition enforcer, has said risks are emerging as dominant companies favour their AI services on their own platforms to exclude rivals.

Technology giants, including OpenAI, Nvidia, Meta and Google have been investing billions on AI infrastructure to cater for booming demand.

data.title: Wall Street mixed after relief rally as Middle East uncertainty lingers
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes: Dow up 0.39%, S&P 500 up 0.20%, Nasdaq down 0.20%
  • Jefferies gains on report Japan's SMFG plans possible takeover
  • Barclays raises year-end target for S&P 500 to 7,650 from 7,400
  • Ares Management, Apollo Global limit redemptions at funds

By Purvi Agarwal and Twesha Dikshit

Wall Street's main indexes were mixed in choppy trading on Tuesday as investors assessed the prospect of easing Middle East tensions, a day after President Donald Trump postponed strikes on Iranian power plants that sparked a relief rally.

Though the indexes opened lower, they pared much of their declines as investors were hopeful of a de-escalation in the Middle East, even as Tehran denied negotiations with the U.S., disputing Trump's comment on "productive talks".

"Investors are trying to wrap their heads around what's going on and where the president is taking this war ... there's a lot of volatility in the market," said Robert Pavlik, senior portfolio manager at Dakota Wealth.

"Investors don't want to be completely out of this market because the president is difficult to figure out ... you could be hurt either being long or being short (in the market)."

Meanwhile, private credit concerns resurfaced after a report said Ares Management limited redemptions at 5% at its private credit fund, along with Apollo Global Management , as withdrawal requests surged. Both firms pared earlier declines and Ares edged higher.

The companies' decisions mirror those of BlackRock and Morgan Stanley earlier this month.

Peers Blackstone and Carlyle dipped 1.3% each.

At 11:53 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 178.17 points, or 0.39%, to 46,386.64, the S&P 500 gained 12.94 points, or 0.20%, to 6,593.94 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 44.61 points, or 0.20%, to 21,902.15.

On the S&P 500, gains in energy and financial stocks helped offset declines in technology and communication services sectors, leaving the index subdued.

A rise in Goldman Sachs and Caterpillar lifted the Dow, while tech stocks weighed on the Nasdaq.

U.S. business activity slowed to an 11-month low in March as the Middle East war raised prices for energy products and other inputs, a survey showed.

On Monday, Wall Street marked its biggest one-day gain since February 6 as investors drew comfort from Trump's comments.

The conflict has driven oil prices sharply higher, reviving inflation jitters and complicating the interest rate outlook for central banks. The U.S. Federal Reserve struck a hawkish tone last week, projecting only one reduction in 2026.

Money markets are no longer pricing in any rate cuts this year, compared with two reductions expected before the Middle East conflict erupted. Expectations for hikes nudged higher amid escalating tensions last week, but were quickly unwound after Trump's comments on Monday, according to CME's FedWatch Tool.

Among individual movers, shares of Jefferies gained 3.7% after the Financial Times reported that Japan's Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is working on plans for a possible takeover of the investment bank.

Cosmetics maker Estee Lauder fell over 9% to an over nine-month low, facing investor backlash as it was in talks for a potential merger with Spanish beauty group Puig Brands .

Barclays lifted its 2026 year-end target for the S&P 500 index to 7,650 from 7,400, citing stronger earnings expectations that outweigh macro risks like Middle East tensions, AI-driven disruption and stress in private credit.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.16-to-1 ratio on the NYSE and by a 1.2-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted 20 new 52-week highs and 17 new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 32 new highs and 129 new lows.

data.title: W.Africa Crude - Nigeria to increase Bonny Light loadings in May
data.description:
Loading programmes for more Nigerian crude oil grades emerged on Tuesday, while high freight rates remained a drag on the market despite supply losses in the Middle East.

  • The Bonny crude stream will load 10 cargoes in May, a copy of the schedule showed, averaging about 318,000 barrels per day in May, up about 19% from the 268,000 bpd in April, while Escravos will load four cargoes and Erha two.

  • There was still an overhang of Nigerian cargoes available for April loading, a trader said, while not all of Angola's crude cargoes for April have traded yet, another trader said on Monday.

  • While freight rates for some West Africa crude shipping routes have stabilised after reaching multi-year highs because of conflict in the Middle East, Riverlake data on LSEG shows they remain elevated.

  • An offer for Nigerian Egina was heard in a range of dated plus $12 to $14 a barrel, Bonny Light was discussed at plus $7 to $8 while Angola's Olombendo was offered at dated Brent plus $13 and Ghana's Sankofa at dated Brent plus $11.50, traders said last week.

  • In the broader market, Nigeria's Dangote Refinery has stepped up gasoline exports across Africa after disruption to energy flows owing to the Middle East conflict. Exports of petroleum products have risen to about 214,000 bpd so far in March, against an average of 100,000 bpd in February, Kpler data showed.

  • Nigeria can increase oil production by about 100,000 bpd over the next few months, said Bashir Bayo Ojulari, group CEO of Nigerian National Petroleum Company on the sidelines of an industry conference in Houston.

data.title: Spire Global Shares Gain on Single-Satellite RF Geolocation
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Spire Global rose after the company said it successfully demonstrated radio-frequency geolocation techniques on a single satellite.

Shares were up 6.9% to $13.71 in midday Tuesday trading. The stock is up nearly 60% over the past 12 months.

While RF geolocation typically relies on sensors on multiple satellites working in conjunction, Spire techniques identify and geolocate radio signals on just one SpaceX satellite, said the space-based provider of data, analytics and other services.

The sensors were also able to detect higher-frequency S-band and X-band signals, which the company on Tuesday said represented an expansion of its overall RF-sensing capabilities.

"Single-satellite RF geolocation allows us to expand the range of signals we can detect from space while improving the efficiency of how those capabilities are deployed," Spire Chief Technology Officer Gabriel Oehme said.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Perma-Fix Environmental Services Slips After Government Shutdown Dampens 4Q Results
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Shares of Perma-Fix Environmental Services slid after the waste-reduction company's fourth-quarter loss widened due to the U.S. government shutdown.

The stock fell 15%, to $10.28, midday Tuesday and is down 18% so far this year.

Perma-Fix's net loss for the quarter widened to $5.7 million, or 31 cents a share, from a loss of $3.5 million, or 22 cents a share, the year before.

While overall revenue increased by $1 million, to $15.7 million, in the fourth quarter, the Atlanta company said its services segment was negatively affected by the government shutdown.

The segment had a 26% decline in revenue year-overy-year primarily due to less project work, Perma-Fix said. The government shutdown in the fall hurt procurement cycles for government-related projects.

Services also had a 42% drop in gross profit and gross margins narrowed, which also was related to the shutdown.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Peloton's Better-Than-Expected Churn Cheered by Bull — Market Talk
data.description: Peloton's churn rate has been better than expected lately, countering investors' fears that higher subscription prices would cause members to quit, UBS analysts say in a note. However, the stock has still been under pressure because the company lowered its revenue guidance for the fiscal year, with sales hurt by lower uptake of new hardware by existing members. "The issue is that product replacement for existing members was never central to the Peloton story," the analysts say. "What has been more central to the story — and a source of doubt for investors — is churn." The analysts say they expect churn to continue to move in the right direction and subscribers to stabilize, allowing Peloton to eventually return to growth. "We see compelling risk/reward," they say, maintaining their $11 price target. Peloton rises 4.7%, to $4.12. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Estee Lauder Shares Continue Slide as Analysts Question Puig Acquisition Talks
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Estee Lauder continued to fall after the company said it is in talks to acquire the Spanish beauty group Puig Brands.

Shares were trading down 10.2% to $71.19 on Tuesday after closing Monday's session in the red at $79.28. The stock is up 9.2% over the past year.

The merger talks, which haven't yet led to an agreement, could unite two of the world's largest beauty companies. The talks come at a pivotal moment for Estee Lauder, which is working to execute a multi-year turnaround to expand its market share and win over younger customers.

Estee Lauder has also been hit hard by U.S. tariffs, with the company projecting a $100 million hit to its profit this year from tariff-related headwinds.

Analysts are unsure that a Puig acquisition would be sufficient to reverse Estee Lauder's woes.

For one, other prospective buyers could emerge for Puig, JPMorgan analysts wrote, now that the market knows that the Puig family would consider giving up control of the storied family business. Even if a deal is reached, the companies may need to reckon with antitrust scrutiny, JPMorgan's analysts write.

There is also substantial integration and execution risk with the merger, given both its size and complexity and Estee Lauder's ongoing turnaround, Morgan Stanley analysts wrote. Puig is more than three times as valuable as Tom Ford was when it agreed in 2022 to be acquired by Estee Lauder in a deal worth about $2.8 billion, which was then the company's largest-ever acquisition.

In recent years, Estee Lauder has struggled with falling sales in Chinese and Korean markets, where former Chief Executive Officer Fabrizio Freda had pushed to expand the company's presence. The company has also faced a tougher overall beauty market as its customer base ages and shoppers across the board become choosier with their purchases.

Estee Lauder brought in Stephane de La Faverie as its new CEO last year, succeeding Freda. Under his leadership, the company has revamped its organization, laying off roughly 7,000 workers so far.

Analysts have said that the turnaround effort - particularly the company's expansion in quickly-growing channels like Amazon and TikTok Shop - is gaining some traction, but they are also looking for further signs of Estee Lauder's execution.

Buying Puig would position Estee Lauder to better compete with L'Oreal, and Puig has strength in complementary geographical regions and verticals, namely fragrances and cosmetics, some analysts said. But it would increase Estee Lauder's exposure to the prestige fragrance and skincare segments, both of which have moved away from peak growth rates, according to analysts at Jeffries.

A sizable deal would also add a layer of complexity to an already involved turnaround process, Morgan Stanley analysts wrote. "In theory, EL on its own without a merger could capture large value upside if it is able to execute its own base business turnaround successfully," they wrote. "Significant M&A could be viewed as a signal of less enthusiasm for EL's confidence in its own turnaround prospects or at least complicate the value opportunity.

And investors may be looking for more fundamental changes at Estee Lauder than just a splashy acquisition, AJ Bell analyst Dan Coatsworth wrote in a note. "A takeover of Puig is an interesting proposition, but history suggests that bolting two companies together is not a guaranteed recipe for success," he wrote.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com
